14: A direct revelation: 'Who gave himself for us to redeem us from all lawlessness and to purify for himself a people of his own possession who are zealous for good works.' The Christological mission-statement of Titus: the self-giving of Christ (edōken heauton) accomplishes two things — ransom from lawlessness and purification-for-possession. The people-of-God language (laos periousios = treasured possession, Exodus 19:5) is deliberately applied to the new covenant community formed by Christ's self-gift. Christ does not merely save individuals but creates a community that embodies his purposes — 'zealous for good works' completes the Christological transaction with an ethical telos.
1: 'But as for you, teach what accords with sound doctrine' — the 'sound doctrine' (hygiainousē didaskalia) that Titus teaches is the Christological proclamation that the surrounding chapters specify: Christ's appearing (Titus 2:11, 13), his self-giving redemption (Titus 2:14), his justifying grace (Titus 3:7). Sound teaching is not primarily an intellectual standard but a Christological one: the doctrine is sound because it is oriented toward the healthy reality of Christ, who is both its content and its goal.
2: 'Older men are to be sober-minded, dignified, self-controlled, sound in faith, in love, and in steadfastness' — the three graces (faith, love, steadfastness) that characterize the mature elder are the character of Christ reproduced in his disciple. Faith is the reception of Christ; love is the Christomorphic disposition of self-giving; steadfastness (hypomonē) is Christ's own patient endurance that believers are directed into (2 Thessalonians 3:5). The aged believer's maturity is a Christological maturity: years of formation in Christ's pattern.
3: 'Older women likewise are to be reverent in behavior, not slanderers or slaves to much wine, but teachers of what is good' — the older women's role as teachers-of-good to younger women is itself a Christological institution: the community shaped by Christ transmits his pattern through its most formed members. The 'reverent in behavior' (hieroprepeis — befitting what is holy) frames their entire demeanor as a form of worship, analogous to the priestly service of the tabernacle. Their teaching role is a ministry of Christ's character formation.
4: 'Train the young women to love their husbands and children' — the domestic loves commanded here are Christologically grounded: the love of husband and children is a form of the self-giving love that Christ models (Ephesians 5:25: husbands, love your wives as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her). The younger women who are trained to love their families embody the Christological love-ethic in the most intimate social contexts. The Titus household-code is not a social conservatism but a Christological formation program for the most proximate relationships.
5: 'To be self-controlled, pure, working at home, kind, and submissive to their own husbands, that the word of God may not be reviled' — the final purpose clause — 'that the word of God may not be reviled' (hina mē ho logos tou theou blasphēmētai) — makes the household conduct of younger women a matter of Christological witness. The word of God they embody is ultimately Christ himself (John 1:1, 14: the Word became flesh). When their conduct is Christomorphic, it protects the credibility of the Christological proclamation they confess.
6: 'Urge the younger men likewise to be self-controlled' — the single command to younger men — self-control — is the comprehensive virtue that encompasses all the rest. In the Platonic tradition, self-control (sōphrosynē) was the virtue that properly ordered the other virtues; in the Christological frame, it is the disposition that comes from being controlled not by one's own passions but by the Spirit of Christ (Galatians 5:22-23: self-control is the final fruit of the Spirit). The younger man who is self-controlled has submitted his energy and drive to Christ's ordering.
7: 'Show yourself in all respects to be a model of good works, and in your teaching show integrity, dignity' — Titus is to be the Christological prototype for the community: the typos (pattern) that Paul describes echoes the same word used of Paul himself as the prototype of grace in 1 Timothy 1:16 (hypotypōsis). The modeling of good works is the Christological pedagogy: the teacher shows the life that Christ makes possible, not only argues for it. Integrity (aphthorian) in teaching is the Christological virtue of being uncorrupted — matching the incorruptible word with incorruptible character.
8: 'And sound speech that cannot be condemned, so that an opponent may be put to shame, having nothing evil to say about us' — the sound speech (logon hygiē) that cannot be condemned is the Christologically shaped proclamation that matches the Christological life. Christ's own opponents were silenced when they could find no charge against him (Matthew 22:46: no one was able to answer him a word); Titus's sound speech, grounded in the same truth, should produce the same silencing effect. The Christological integrity of word and life together is the apologetic.
9: 'Bondservants are to be submissive to their own masters in everything; they are to be well-pleasing, not argumentative' — the instruction to enslaved persons has Christological grounding in the servant-pattern of Christ himself (Philippians 2:7: he took on the form of a servant; Mark 10:45: the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve). The submission to masters is not an endorsement of the institution but an application of the Christological servant-pattern to the most constrained social location: even there, the Christ-pattern of faithful service is possible.
10: 'Not stealing, but showing all good faith, so that in everything they may adorn the doctrine of God our Savior' — the 'adorning' of the doctrine (kosmosin tēn didaskalian tēs tou theou sōtēros hēmōn) is the Christological motivation for enslaved persons' faithful conduct: their trustworthiness makes the God who saved them visible and attractive. The doctrine of God our Savior is adorned not by theological argument but by the embodied character of those who have been saved by it. The enslaved person who models integrity is a Christological exhibit.
11: 'For the grace of God has appeared (epephanē), bringing salvation to all people' — the appearing (epiphaneia) of grace is the Incarnation: grace is not an attribute of God abstractly considered but the person of Christ made visible in history. The grace that 'appeared' is Christ himself; the salvation it 'brings' is the salvation Christ accomplished. The universal scope ('to all people,' pasin anthrōpois) grounds all the preceding household-code instructions: the social transformation of household relationships is made possible by the universally-offered grace that appeared in Christ.
12: 'Training us to renounce ungodliness and worldly passions, and to live self-controlled, upright, and godly lives in the present age' — the grace that appeared (Christ) functions pedagogically: it 'trains' (paideuousa) believers in the same way a master trains a pupil. Christ is not only the object of faith but the teacher of the new life that faith produces. The training in self-control, uprightness, and godliness is Christological formation: being shaped into the moral character that Christ embodies and the Spirit produces.
13: A direct revelation: 'Waiting for our blessed hope, the appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ.' The syntax — 'our great God and Savior Jesus Christ' (tou megalou theou kai sōtēros hēmōn Iēsou Christou) — with a single definite article governing both nouns is one of the most explicit divine predications of Jesus in the entire NT (the Granville Sharp rule). Paul calls Jesus 'our great God' directly; the eschatological appearing of Christ is simultaneously the appearing of the glory of God himself. The Christological confession reaches its highest point in the letter here.
15: 'Declare these things; exhort and rebuke with all authority. Let no one disregard you' — the authority Titus wields in declaring, exhorting, and rebuking is Christ's authority mediated through the apostolic commission. Christ gave the apostolic commission with 'all authority in heaven and on earth' (Matthew 28:18-20); the minister who acts in Christ's name acts with that authority. The instruction 'let no one disregard you' parallels the instruction to Timothy about his youth (1 Timothy 4:12) — the authority is not personal but Christological, and therefore not subject to social dismissal.
